title: 7 3 The shifting theorems

7.3 THE SHIFTING THEOREMS

LAPLACE TRANSFORMS

We turn now to two central theorems (both of which appear in your standard table) which will allow us to cope with shifts in both the s and the t variables. These results are handy when finding some particular Laplace transforms and are absolutely essential when dealing with inverse Laplace transforms.

First Shifting Theorem

L(e‚ąíat f (t)) = F (s + a) Proof:

What the first shifting theorem is saying is that the impact of an e‚ąíat in the t variable is to shift s to s + a in the s variable. We use the first shifting theorem in both directions!

Second Shifting Theorem

L(f (t ‚ąí c)u(t ‚ąí c)) = e‚ąícs F (s) Proof:

What the second shifting theorem is saying is that shifting and cutting in the t variable introduces an exponential function in the s variable.

Example 4 Let f (t) = sin(t ‚ąí ŌÄ)u(t ‚ąí ŌÄ). Sketch a graph of f and find its Laplace transform.

Example 5 Let f (t) = t2 u(t ‚ąí 3). Sketch a graph of f and find its Laplace transform.

Sketch:

We have a Heaviside cut here but no shift ! So let‚Äôs generate a shift of 3 units by writing

t2 = a(t ‚ąí 3)2 + b(t ‚ąí 3) + c. We want to find the values of a, b and c that make both sides balance. We could do this by expanding the right-hand side and matching powers of t, but we also have a nice trick using calculus:

So t2 = (t ‚ąí 3)2 + 6(t ‚ąí 3) + 9 and hence our problem is to find the Laplace transform of

{(t ‚ąí 3)2 + 6(t ‚ąí 3) + 9}u(t ‚ąí 3) = (t ‚ąí 3)2 u(t ‚ąí 3) + 6(t ‚ąí 3)u(t ‚ąí 3) + 9u(t ‚ąí 3).
